pi_items.metadata_value: Real 2022 Iowa county corn yields from USDA NASS (Feb 24 2023); ISU Extension district benchmarks for potential yields
pi_items.verified_value: Real 2022 Iowa county corn yields from USDA NASS; ISU Extension district benchmarks; 22 Iowa counties included
pi_items.source: Examined iowa_corn_yield.xlsx workbook (County_Data sheet), web search confirms USDA NASS published 2022 Iowa county estimates on Feb 24 2023, Iowa State University Extension provides district benchmarks
Web search confirmed this URL is a valid USDA NASS publication for 2022 Iowa Corn County Estimates
USDA NASS web search result confirms Iowa's 2022 corn yield state average is 200.0 bushels per acre
Calculated from 22 counties in iowa_corn_yield.xlsx workbook: SUMPRODUCT(Potential-Actual per county, harvested area) / total harvested area = 11.33%
Expected value 11.33% falls within stated range [10.0, 12.5]
Threshold used in IF formula (Potential - Actual)/Potential * 100 >= 13.0 for 'High Gap' category
Threshold used in IF formula for 'Low Gap' category when gap < 9.0%

Calculated from 22 counties: 7 counties have yield gap >= 13% (Pottawattamie 18.75%, Adair 17.90%, Cass 16.65%, Chickasaw 14.16%, Clayton 13.84%, Allamakee 13.64%, Muscatine 13.37%). Metadata claim is minimum; actual count exceeds it.
Calculated from 22 counties: 5 counties have yield gap < 9% (Clinton 8.46%, Cerro Gordo 7.70%, Delaware 7.68%, Mitchell 7.28%, Franklin 6.34%). Metadata claim is minimum; actual count exceeds it.
Calculated yield gaps for all 22 counties from workbook; Pottawattamie County has highest gap at (240-195)/240*100 = 18.75%
Calculated from Pottawattamie County data: actual 195.0 bu/acre, potential 240 bu/acre, gap = (240-195)/240*100 = 18.75%
Calculated yield gaps for all 22 counties from workbook; Franklin County has lowest gap at (235-220.1)/235*100 = 6.34%
Calculated from Franklin County data: actual 220.1 bu/acre, potential 235 bu/acre, gap = (235-220.1)/235*100 = 6.34%

privileged_info_summary: The task uses real 2022 Iowa corn yield data from USDA NASS (state average 200.0 bu/acre, published Feb 24 2023) across 22 selected Iowa counties. The workbook contains actual county yields, ISU Extension district potential yield benchmarks, and harvested acreage. All computed yield gap statistics are verified: weighted average gap is 11.33% (range 10.0-12.5%), with 7 counties having high yield gaps (≥13%) and 5 counties having low gaps (<9%). Pottawattamie County has the highest gap (18.75%, rank 22) and Franklin County has the lowest (6.34%, rank 1). Data is real, not synthetic.
